rows.v: An interval is the distance between two pitches. The unison is the same note; the octave is the next note of the same name, eight scale steps up.
An octave doubles the frequency — play A at 440 Hz and the A above is 880 Hz. Our ears hear that 2:1 ratio as 'the same note, higher,' which is why both are called A.
Octaves thicken a melody and add power without changing the harmony. Doubling a bass line an octave up is one of the oldest tricks for fullness.
Somewhere Over the Rainbow opens with an octave leap on 'Some-where' — the single most recognizable octave in music.
A third spans three scale steps. The major third is bright and happy; the minor third is darker and sadder. This one interval decides a chord's whole mood.
A major third is four half-steps; a minor third is three. That single half-step difference is the line between a major and a minor chord — the emotional heart of Western harmony.
Stack thirds to build every basic chord. Want a sadder version of any major chord? Lower its third by a half-step.
The major third opens 'Oh When the Saints'; the minor third is the 'Greensleeves' / 'Stairway' melancholy color.
The interval spanning five scale steps — the most stable and consonant interval after the octave, and the backbone of the circle of fifths.
Its frequencies sit in a clean 3:2 ratio, which the ear hears as open and strong. It's so stable it sounds almost hollow on its own.
Fifths anchor harmony and define a key's home (tonic) and away (dominant). Rock power chords are nothing but stacked fifths.
The Star Wars main theme leaps a perfect fifth; 'Twinkle, Twinkle' jumps one between its first two notes.
The interval of four scale steps — strong and consonant, the inversion of the perfect fifth. Stable but with a slight pull to resolve.
A 4:3 frequency ratio. Flip a perfect fifth upside down and you get a perfect fourth; the two are mirror images within the octave.
Fourths give a bold, open, sometimes heroic sound. Quartal harmony (chords built in fourths) is a staple of modern jazz.
'Here Comes the Bride' and the opening of 'Amazing Grace' both rise a perfect fourth.
The interval splitting the octave exactly in half — three whole steps. The most dissonant, unstable interval, historically nicknamed 'the devil in music.'
It sits at the awkward midpoint with no simple frequency ratio, so the ear hears maximum tension. That tension is exactly what makes it useful.
The tritone inside a dominant-seventh chord is what creates the urge to resolve home. Tension you can deploy on purpose.
'Maria' from West Side Story opens on a tritone; it's also the wailing two-note siren of The Simpsons theme.
Larger intervals: the sixth is sweet and lyrical; the seventh is tense and yearning, just shy of the octave.
Sixths are mild consonances that sound rich and warm. Sevenths are dissonances that strongly want to step up to the octave, creating forward motion.
Sixths make lovely harmony lines for two singers or guitars. Sevenths add the color and tension that define jazz chords.
'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean' opens on a sixth; the major-seventh stretch colors countless jazz ballads.
Consonance is the quality of intervals that sound stable and at rest; dissonance is tension that wants to resolve. The push and pull between them drives all music.
Simple frequency ratios (octave 2:1, fifth 3:2) sound consonant; complex ones (tritone, seconds, sevenths) sound dissonant. But it's also cultural — what counts as 'harsh' has shifted over centuries.
Music breathes by moving from tension to release. Set up dissonance, then resolve it to consonance — that arc is the basic engine of emotional effect.
Every suspension in a hymn, every bluesy clash, every jazz chord that 'wants' to move is consonance and dissonance at work.
The seven-note scale that sounds bright and 'complete' — do-re-mi-fa-sol-la-ti-do. The reference scale for nearly all Western music.
Its pattern of whole and half steps is fixed: W-W-H-W-W-W-H. That exact sequence is what makes any major scale sound major, no matter which note you start on.
It's the foundation everything else is measured against. Learn it in every key and most other theory falls into place.
'Do-Re-Mi' from The Sound of Music literally teaches it; 'Joy to the World' is a major scale descending.
The basic minor scale — darker and more somber than major. The 'sad' scale, also called the Aeolian mode.
It flattens the 3rd, 6th, and 7th degrees of the major scale (pattern W-H-W-W-H-W-W). Those three lowered notes are the source of its melancholy.
Reach for it whenever you want a sad, serious, or moody feel. It shares all its notes with its relative major.
'Greensleeves,' 'Scarborough Fair,' and a huge amount of folk and metal live in natural minor.
A minor scale with a raised 7th, giving an exotic, dramatic flavor and a strong pull back to the tonic.
Raising the 7th creates a leading tone that resolves strongly home, but it also opens an unusual step-and-a-half gap that gives the scale its Middle-Eastern/Spanish color.
Use it when minor-key harmony needs a strong dominant chord, or when you want that dramatic, slightly exotic sound.
The 'Sherlock'/spy-movie tension sound and much flamenco and classical minor-key music use harmonic minor.
A minor scale that raises both the 6th and 7th going up, then often reverts to natural minor coming down — built to make minor-key melodies flow smoothly.
The raised 6th and 7th smooth out the awkward leap of harmonic minor when a melody is ascending. Composers traditionally lowered them again on the way down.
A go-to for graceful minor-key melody writing, and (in its ascending form) a core scale in jazz improvisation.
Bach's minor-key melodies and a great deal of jazz soloing draw on melodic minor.
A five-note scale that sounds good over almost anything — the friendliest scale in music, with no 'wrong' notes.
It removes the two most tension-prone notes from the major or minor scale, leaving five that consistently sound consonant together. That's why it's nearly foolproof.
The first scale to learn for improvising. Solo over a blues or pop song with the matching pentatonic and you'll rarely hit a clashing note.
Countless guitar solos, 'Amazing Grace,' 'My Girl,' and folk music worldwide are pentatonic.
The pentatonic minor scale plus one extra note — the 'blue note' — that gives blues, rock, and jazz their gritty, expressive sound.
Adding a flatted fifth (the blue note) between the 4th and 5th creates a sliding, dissonant tension that's the signature of the blues.
The default vocabulary for blues and rock soloing. Bend into and out of the blue note for that crying, vocal quality.
Every blues guitar solo, plus the swagger of countless rock riffs, leans on the blues scale.
Seven scales made by starting the major scale on each of its different notes — the same notes, but a different 'home,' giving each a distinct mood.
Start C major on D and you get D Dorian; on E, E Phrygian, and so on. Same seven notes, but the new tonic shifts which intervals fall where, changing the color completely.
Modes let you stay in one set of notes while conjuring very different moods — Dorian's cool jazz, Phrygian's Spanish edge, Lydian's dreamy lift.
'So What' (Dorian), Spanish flamenco (Phrygian), and The Simpsons theme (Lydian) showcase the modes.
The scale of all twelve notes — every half-step within the octave, with no gaps.
It includes every pitch Western music uses, so it has no key center of its own. It's raw material rather than a 'home' scale.
Use chromatic runs and passing tones to connect other notes smoothly and add tension, color, or a sense of slithering motion.
'Flight of the Bumblebee' is almost pure chromatic motion; jazz fills are full of chromatic passing notes.
The basic three-note chord, built by stacking two thirds. Major, minor, diminished, and augmented triads are the building blocks of harmony.
Take a root, add the note a third above, then another third on top. The size of those thirds (major or minor) decides the chord's quality and mood.
Triads are the harmonic vocabulary of most songs. Knowing the four types and how to build them on any note covers an enormous amount of music.
Nearly every pop, folk, and rock song is built on major and minor triads.
A triad with one more third stacked on top, adding a 4th note (the seventh). Richer and more colorful than a plain triad.
The added seventh introduces mild tension and sophistication. Dominant sevenths in particular create a strong pull to resolve, driving harmony forward.
Sevenths are the entry point to jazzier, more emotional harmony. Swap a plain chord for its seventh to instantly add color.
The bluesy dominant sevenths of every blues tune, and the lush major sevenths of jazz and R&B ballads.
The same chord with a note other than the root in the bass — same notes, different bottom, different feel.

Move the root up an octave and the third sits in the bass (first inversion); move the third up too and the fifth is in the bass (second inversion). The harmony is unchanged, but the color and smoothness shift.
Inversions let the bass line move smoothly between chords instead of leaping, and they vary the texture of repeated chords.
Pachelbel's Canon and countless hymns use inversions to create a stepwise, flowing bass.
Chords stacked beyond the seventh — adding the 9th, 11th, or 13th for lush, complex color.
Keep stacking thirds above a seventh chord and you reach the 9th, 11th, and 13th. Each added note brings more richness and ambiguity.
These are the sophisticated colors of jazz, funk, soul, and film scoring. Use them where a plain chord would feel too simple.
The shimmering chords of jazz, the funk of Stevie Wonder, and lush film scores all use extensions.
A chord where the third is replaced by the note just above or below it (the 4th or 2nd), creating an open, unresolved sound that 'hangs.'
With no third, a sus chord is neither major nor minor — it sits in suspense, usually wanting to resolve to the regular chord.
Use sus chords to delay resolution and build gentle tension, then drop to the plain chord for release. Great for intros and atmosphere.
The opening of 'Pinball Wizard' and the jangle of countless folk-rock songs lean on sus chords.
Just the root and the fifth — no third — so it's neither major nor minor. The raw, heavy backbone of rock and metal.
Dropping the third removes the major/minor flavor and any harmonic 'sweetness,' leaving a thick, neutral sound that holds up under distortion.
Play them on a distorted electric guitar for power and aggression. Their neutrality lets them sit over any harmony.
Virtually every rock and metal riff — from 'Smoke on the Water' to punk — is built on power chords.
Two tense, unstable triads: the diminished (two stacked minor thirds) sounds dark and anxious; the augmented (two major thirds) sounds eerie and unresolved.
Both break the comfortable major/minor mold. Diminished chords are full of tritone tension; augmented chords stretch the fifth wider until it floats.
Use them as tension chords that demand resolution, or as colorful passing chords between stable ones. Staples of suspense and surprise.
Horror and suspense scores lean on diminished chords; the dreamy unease of augmented chords colors many film and jazz moments.
The three-chord backbone of Western music — the I, IV, and V chords of a key. With these alone you can play thousands of songs.
These are the three major chords built on the 1st, 4th, and 5th degrees of a scale. Together they cover every note of the key, so almost any melody fits over them.
The first progression to learn. Master I–IV–V in a few keys and you can play along with an enormous swath of folk, rock, country, and pop.
'Twist and Shout,' 'La Bamba,' 'Wild Thing,' and most early rock 'n' roll are I–IV–V.
The most important progression in jazz — a smooth, satisfying path home through the ii, V, and I chords.
The ii leads naturally to the V (a fifth away), which has a strong pull to the I. Each chord sets up the next, creating an irresistible sense of arrival.
Recognize and practice ii–V–I in every key and you've unlocked the grammar of jazz standards. It's the progression that ties countless tunes together.
'Autumn Leaves,' 'Fly Me to the Moon,' and most jazz standards are built from chains of ii–V–I.
The strongest way to end a phrase — V moving to I — that sounds fully resolved, like a musical period at the end of a sentence.
The dominant (V) chord contains the tension-laden leading tone and tritone that pull hard toward the tonic (I). Landing on I releases all that tension: home at last.
Use it to end sections and songs with a sense of finality. It's the default 'the end' in tonal music.
The decisive final chords of nearly every classical piece and hymn.
The 'Amen' cadence — IV moving to I — a softer, more peaceful resolution than the authentic cadence.
Moving from the subdominant (IV) to the tonic (I) resolves gently, without the sharp leading-tone pull of a V–I. The effect is calm and settled rather than emphatic.
Use it for a gentle, reverent, or relaxed ending — anywhere a full authentic cadence would feel too forceful.
The 'A-men' sung at the end of hymns is the textbook plagal cadence.
A surprise ending — the V chord sets you up to land on I, but resolves somewhere unexpected (usually vi) instead.
The ear expects V to resolve to I. Substituting the vi chord (which shares two notes with I) gives a soft landing that's a surprise without being jarring — resolution deferred.
Use it to extend a phrase, dodge a too-early ending, or create a moment of wistful surprise before the real cadence arrives.
Composers from Bach to film scorers use deceptive cadences to keep a piece from ending before its time.
A 12-measure chord pattern using I, IV, and V — the form underneath most blues and a huge amount of early rock 'n' roll.
It arranges the three primary chords into a fixed 12-bar cycle with a predictable shape, leaving room for endless melodic and lyrical variation over the same frame.
Learn the 12-bar pattern and you can jam with blues musicians anywhere — it's a shared language. A perfect frame for practicing soloing.
'Johnny B. Goode,' 'Hound Dog,' and the entire blues tradition run on the 12-bar form.
A chord progression that moves by descending fifths — following the circle of fifths — producing a powerfully natural sense of forward motion.
Each chord sits a fifth below the last, so every change feels like the strongest possible resolution, chained one after another. The ear is pulled continuously forward.
Use a circle progression when you want momentum and inevitability, or to travel smoothly through several chords back to home.
'Hey Joe,' 'I Will Survive,' and the turnarounds of countless jazz tunes use circle-of-fifths motion.
The beat is the steady pulse you tap your foot to; tempo is how fast that pulse goes, measured in beats per minute (BPM).
The beat is the underlying grid of time; tempo sets its speed. A slow 60 BPM is one beat a second; a fast dance track might run 128 BPM or more.
Lock to the beat to play in time, and choose a tempo that fits the feel — slow for ballads, fast for energy. A metronome trains both.
Every song you can clap along to is built on a beat; the difference between a lullaby and a dance track is largely tempo.
The two stacked numbers at the start of music that tell you how beats are grouped — like 4/4, 3/4, or 6/8.
The top number is how many beats per measure; the bottom is which note value gets the beat. 4/4 is four quarter-notes a bar; 3/4 is the three-beat waltz feel.
Knowing the time signature tells you where the strong beats fall, so you can feel the groove and place accents correctly.
4/4 powers nearly all pop and rock; 3/4 is every waltz; 6/8 gives the lilt of many ballads and jigs.
The symbols showing how long each note lasts — whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth — each half the length of the one before.
It's a halving system: a whole note splits into two halves, a half into two quarters, and so on. Dots and ties extend these basic durations.
Reading note values lets you play rhythms accurately from a page and count complex passages by subdividing the beat.
The steady quarter-notes of a march versus the rapid sixteenths of a drum fill are note values at work.
Rhythm that accents the off-beats — the 'and' between the main beats — creating a sense of surprise, groove, and momentum.
By placing stresses where the ear expects rest (and vice versa), syncopation works against the steady pulse, which is exactly what makes it feel lively and propulsive.
Use syncopation to make a rhythm groove instead of plod. It's the difference between stiff and funky.
Ragtime, funk, reggae's off-beat skank, and most catchy pop hooks are deeply syncopated.
A rhythmic feel where pairs of notes are played long-short instead of even, giving jazz and blues their characteristic bounce.
Instead of dividing the beat into two equal halves, swing stretches the first and shortens the second (roughly a 2:1 lilt), creating a rolling, triplet-based groove.
Apply swing to straight eighth-notes to instantly turn a stiff line jazzy. It's felt more than counted — you learn it by listening.
Big-band jazz, blues shuffles, and 'It Don't Mean a Thing (If It Ain't Got That Swing)' define the feel.
Two conflicting rhythms played at once — like three beats against two — creating rich, complex, often hypnotic textures.
Each rhythm keeps its own pulse, and the ear hears both grids at once. Where they line up and pull apart creates the characteristic tension and motion.
Use polyrhythm to add depth and sophistication, layering a contrasting rhythm over the main groove. Central to African and much modern music.
West African drumming, much of Steve Reich's music, and progressive rock and metal thrive on polyrhythm.
The numbered positions within a scale (1 through 7), each with a name and a characteristic 'pull' — like the restful tonic or the restless leading tone.
Every note of a key has a role: the 1st (tonic) is home, the 5th (dominant) is the main tension, the 7th (leading tone) strains upward to resolve. These roles, not the letter names, drive harmony.
Thinking in scale degrees (or Roman numerals) lets you understand and transpose music in any key — the relationships stay the same even when the notes change.
Solfège ('do-re-mi') is scale degrees by another name; it's how singers internalize a key.
The two harmonic poles of a key: the tonic (I) is home and rest; the dominant (V) is tension that pulls back to it. The basic gravity of tonal music.
The dominant chord contains the leading tone and a tritone, both straining toward the tonic. That built-in tension-and-release is the engine of nearly all Western harmony.
Set up the dominant, then resolve to the tonic, to create satisfying arrival. Almost every phrase and cadence relies on this relationship.
The 'away then home' feeling at the end of virtually every classical phrase and pop chorus.
The chords built using only the notes of one key — the seven chords that 'belong' together and form the harmonic palette of most songs.
Stack thirds on each scale degree and you get a fixed set of chord qualities (in major: I, ii, iii, IV, V, vi, vii°). These are the chords that naturally fit a key.
Knowing a key's diatonic chords tells you which chords will sound 'right' together, so you can build progressions and harmonize melodies confidently.
Most pop songs use only diatonic chords; the famous I–V–vi–IV pop progression is pure diatonic harmony.
Changing from one key to another within a piece — shifting the harmonic home to create contrast, lift, or drama.
A modulation usually pivots through a chord shared by both keys, then confirms the new home with its own dominant. Done smoothly, the ear is led somewhere new without feeling lost.
Use modulation for a fresh-energy key change in a final chorus, or to give a longer piece structural variety and travel.
The triumphant final-chorus key-bump in countless pop songs, and the elaborate modulations of classical sonatas.
The art of moving each individual melodic line (voice) smoothly from chord to chord, usually by small steps rather than leaps.
Good voice-leading keeps common tones held, moves other voices to the nearest available note, and avoids awkward parallels. The chords connect like interlocking gears.
Apply it to make chord progressions sound smooth and professional, and to write inner harmony parts that are satisfying to sing or play.
The seamless part-writing of Bach chorales is the gold standard of voice-leading.
A chord borrowed from outside the key that acts as the dominant of a chord other than the tonic — a temporary detour that adds color and pull.
It's a 'V of' something: a dominant chord aimed at a chord that isn't the key's home, momentarily treating that chord as a temporary tonic before resolving to it.
Use secondary dominants to add richness and forward drive to an otherwise plain diatonic progression — a splash of harmonic spice.
The surprise bright chord that 'leads somewhere' in jazz standards and sophisticated pop is often a secondary dominant.
The five-line staff where notes are written, and the clefs (treble, bass) that fix which pitches the lines and spaces represent.
A note's vertical position on the staff shows its pitch; the clef at the start anchors the whole system — treble for higher instruments and voices, bass for lower.
Reading the staff and clefs is the entry point to sheet music. The mnemonics ('Every Good Boy…', 'FACE') get you started fast.
Any piece of sheet music you've ever seen relies on the staff and a clef.
The group of sharps or flats at the start of each line that tells you which key the music is in, so you don't mark every accidental individually.
The number and pattern of sharps or flats maps directly to a key (1 sharp = G major, 2 flats = B♭ major, and so on). It's shorthand drawn straight from the circle of fifths.
Read the key signature first to know which notes are consistently sharp or flat, and to identify the key (and its likely chords) at a glance.
Every printed score opens with a key signature; recognizing them is half of sight-reading.
The markings that tell you how loud or soft to play — from pianissimo (very soft) to fortissimo (very loud) — and how to shade between them.
Italian terms and symbols set relative volume (p, f) and gradual change (crescendo to grow, decrescendo to fade). They shape the emotional contour of a performance.
Use dynamics to give music life and direction — swelling into a climax, pulling back for intimacy. Flat dynamics make even great notes feel lifeless.
The hushed-to-thunderous swells of a film score or symphony are dynamics doing the emotional heavy lifting.
The most common song form in popular music: alternating verses (same music, new words) with a repeating chorus (the memorable hook).
Verses advance the story while the chorus delivers the central, repeated emotional payoff. A bridge often adds contrast before a final chorus. The structure balances novelty and familiarity.
Use it as the default blueprint for writing a catchy song: verse, chorus, verse, chorus, bridge, chorus.
The overwhelming majority of pop, rock, and country hits follow verse–chorus form.
A 32-bar song form in four 8-bar sections: a main melody (A) repeated, a contrasting bridge (B), then the main melody again. The backbone of the Great American Songbook.
Three statements of the A section frame a single contrasting B section (the 'middle eight'). The repetition makes the tune memorable; the bridge keeps it from growing stale.
Reach for AABA when writing in a classic, standards-style idiom, or to give a melody a satisfying, balanced shape.
'Somewhere Over the Rainbow,' 'I Got Rhythm,' and most jazz standards are AABA.
A large-scale classical form in three parts — exposition, development, recapitulation — that presents themes, explores them, then brings them home.
The exposition states two contrasting themes in different keys; the development fragments and transforms them through distant keys; the recapitulation returns them, now reconciled in the home key.
Understanding sonata form is the key to following the architecture of most classical first movements — knowing where you are in the journey.
The opening movements of Mozart, Haydn, and Beethoven symphonies and sonatas are built on sonata form.
